coredata.dc:title: Surface chemistry tuning the selectivity of carbon nanodots towards Hg2+ recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Mengli
Liu, Cui
Peng, Ying
Xiao, Ru-Zhen
Zhang, Song
Zhang, Zhi-Ling
Zhang, Bing
Pang, Dai-Wen
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence quenching of carbon nanodots by metal ions has been extensively applied for the determination of oligonucleotides, proteins, small molecules and metal ions. However, the problem of poor selectivity originating from the coordination of surface oxygen-containing groups to many kinds of metal ions has limited the prosperity of carbon nanodots in detection field. Herein, the specific recognition of carbon nanodots to Hg2+ is controlled by rational regulation of the surface structure of carbon nanodots. Passivation of the surface carboxyl and hydroxyl groups plays a decisive role in inhibiting the binding of metal ions with carbon nanodots. Upon the attachment of Hg2+ specific recognition unit, carbon nanodots exhibited a high selectivity to Hg2+. This work facilitates to rationally design the surface structure of carbon nanodots to obtain the desirable selective recognition ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface chemistry
Carbon nanodots
Selectivity
Recognition
Hg2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dots and C-dots inhibited by 1,3-propane sultone (iC-dot)
Preparation of C-dots modified by dibenzocyclooctyne-amine and 1,3-propane sultone (sC-dots)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Transmission electron microscopy of the as-prepared C-dots (the inset is the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy of C-dots). (B) The statistical size analysis of the C-dots by transmission electron microscopy. (C) The high resolution C 1s spectra and (D) Raman spectra analysis of C-dots. (E) Effect of excitation wavelengths on the fluorescence emission peaks of C-dots. (F) Effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. The concentration of each metal ions is 1 mM.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of C-dots (purple), C-dots with Hg2+ (orange), C-dots with Hg2+ and EDTA (magenta). (B) The femtosecond transient absorption of C-dots only and C-dots with Hg2+ at 525 nm. The transient absorption spectra analysis of (C) C-dots only and (D) C-dots with Hg2+ within delay time of 50 ps.
(A) Effects of metal ions on the fluorescence of iC-dots, the concentration of each metal ions were 1 mM. (B) Effects of Hg2+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots and iC-dots.

(A) The fourier transform infrared and (B) XPS survey of C-dots and sC-dots. (C)The proton magnetic resonance spectra of C-dots and sC-dots. (D) Effects of metal ions on the fluorescence of sC-dots, the concentration of each these metal ions was 1 mM. (E) The selectivity of our proposed sC-dots-DNA towards metal ions detection. The concentration of Hg2+ was 0.1 mM, and the concentrations of the other metal ions were 1 mM.
Diagram of surface engineering process for enhancing selectivity of C-dots towards Hg2+ recognition response.
The selectivity coefficient of different metal ions towards Hg2+ before and after the surface change of C-dots.
Comparison of the performance for Hg2+ detection using nanomaterials as a probe.
The detection of Hg2+ in natural samples by the standard addition method.

head.ce:title: Surface chemistry tuning the selectivity of carbon nanodots towards Hg2+ recognition

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Wuhan Institute of Physics and Mathematics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence quenching of carbon nanodots by metal ions has been extensively applied for the determination of oligonucleotides, proteins, small molecules and metal ions. However, the problem of poor selectivity originating from the coordination of surface oxygen-containing groups to many kinds of metal ions has limited the prosperity of carbon nanodots in detection field. Herein, the specific recognition of carbon nanodots to Hg2+ is controlled by rational regulation of the surface structure of carbon nanodots. Passivation of the surface carboxyl and hydroxyl groups plays a decisive role in inhibiting the binding of metal ions with carbon nanodots. Upon the attachment of Hg2+ specific recognition unit, carbon nanodots exhibited a high selectivity to Hg2+. This work facilitates to rationally design the surface structure of carbon nanodots to obtain the desirable selective recognition ability.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) with fascinating features such as biocompatible, resistance to photobleaching, and good stability have sparked intensive research efforts [1–4]. The favorable photophysical and chemical properties of C-dots make them popular in sensing [5–10], imaging [11–13], catalysis [14–16] and optoelectronics [17–19]. Over years, much attention has been paid for various kinds of metal ions detections by C-dots [20–23]. Liu et al. proposed a trace Cu2+ detection by the obtained C-dots based on the coordination of Cu2+ with –COOH and –NH2 groups [24]. Lu et al. showed that Fe3+ ions could dynamic quench the fluorescence of C-dots synthesized from one-step hydrothermal reaction [22]. Huang et al. noticed that the fluorescence of C-dots synthesized from pyrolysis of spermine and citric acid were highly quenched by Hg2+ [25]. Despite many progress has been made in the past, due to the complex of structure, C-dots still have much room for improvement in the ion recognition selectivity aspect. Generally, C-dots with different structures display different preference for metal ions. It is inefficient and blind for careful selection of suitable synthetic precursor and synthesis parameters to obtain C-dots with high selectivity to specific metal ions. Therefore, it is essential to explore a universal method to improve the selective discrimination of C-dots to metal ions through simple structural regulation.

Here, Hg2+ taken as an ion example, surface chemistry strategy was applied to improve the selective recognition of C-dots towards Hg2+. Due to the coordination of interfering metal ions with surface functional groups of C-dots [26–30], the problem of low selective recognition of C-dots towards Hg2+ is not addressed. In this work, these functional groups were passivated to inhibit the binding of metal ions, thereby inhibiting fluorescence quenching of C-dots from the interfering ions. On the basis of interference reduce, linkage of T-rich DNA on C-dots make the highly selective recognition of Hg2+ came true. Moreover, compared with the reports focusing on searching suitable precursors, reaction conditions, and post-processing methods, our method proposed is more universal to the selectivity improvement of metal ions, as long as the corresponding recognition molecules were selected, such as DNA with G-rich sequence for selective recognition of Pb2+, C-rich DNA for Ag+, and the Cu2+-mediated hydroxypyridone base pair for selective Cu2+ recognition [31–33].

The carbon fiber powder was obtained from Shanghai Tansu Manufactory. Nitric acid (65–68%), sulfuric acid (98%), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), 1,4-dioxane, acetonitrile, cupric chloride (CuCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), silver nitrate (AgNO3), lead chloride (PbCl2), manganese(II) chloride (MnCl2), bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3), mercuric nitrate (Hg(NO3)2) were purchased from local supplies. Dibenzocyclooctyne-amine (DBCO-NH2) was purchased from Shanghai GeorGene Biotechnology Co. Ltd, triethylamine was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, 1,3-propane sultone was purchased from Energy Chemical. The ultrapure water was purified using a Synergy ultrapure water system (Millipore). The mercury-specific oligonucleotide sequence (5′-N3-TTTTTTTTCCCTTTTTTTT-3′) was synthesized and HPLC-purified by Sangon Inc. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots and C-dots inhibited by 1,3-propane sultone (iC-dot)
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots were synthesized from top-down method [34,35]. The solution component of <3 KDa was freeze-dried to powder for further use. Based on the reaction of carboxyl and hydroxyl with 1,3-propane sultone [36,37], the –COOH and –OH groups of C-dots were inhibited by 1,3-propane sultone, and the products were abbreviated as iC-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of C-dots modified by dibenzocyclooctyne-amine and 1,3-propane sultone (sC-dots)
ce:section.ce:para: 3 mg of C-dots were acyl chlorinated and reacted with dibenzocyclooctyne-amine (DBCO-NH2). After rotary evaporating, 10 mL of 1,4-dioxane was dropped. Subsequently, 0.25 g of 1,3-propane sultone was added with 400 μL of trimethylamine, stirred in 40 °C water bath for 24 h. In the following, the products were purified by the dialysis and abbreviated as sC-dots.

Proper amount of sC-dots was added to DNA-N3 at room temperature for 24 h to complete the cycloaddition click chemistry reaction (the mass ratio of DNA and sC-dots was 1:10). Afterwards, the products of sC-dots-DNA were separated and purified by agarose gel electrophoresis.

The C-dots in this work was prepared from the modified top-down method [34]. The transmission electron microscopy image of C-dots was displayed in Fig. 1 A. It indicated that the C-dots was uniform and monodisperse, with a mean diameter of 2.1 ± 0.7 nm (Fig. 1B). Inset of Fig. 1A showed the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy of C-dots, and it indicates a lattice spacing of 100 facet of the graphite carbon. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was monitored to explore the surface structure of C-dots. As presented in Fig. 1C, the high resolution C 1s spectrum was peak fitted to graphitic carbons, alcoholic carbons, carbonyl carbon, and carboxyl carbons. The corresponding content of these groups were calculated to be 58.4%, 17.1%, 17.8% and 6.7%, respectively (Table S1). The Raman spectrum of C-dots displayed in Fig. 1D shows two distinct bands, which is attributed to the disordered structure and graphitic structure of carbon materials (D band located at 1375 cm-1 and G band located at 1595 cm-1). The Raman intensity ratio of I D/I G is 0.95, implying a large amount of defects existing on C-dots. The fluorescence emission wavelength of C-dots behaves excitation wavelength independent (Fig. 1E). The responses of C-dots to different kinds of metal ions were investigated in Fig. 1F. With the existence of Hg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Bi3+ and Ag+, the quenched fluorescence of C-dots was obviously seen. This phenomenon has been commonly reported in literatures [26–29]. It is the strong coordination effect between the surface structures of C-dots and metal ions that make the selective identification of a specific ion by C-dots rather difficult.

As we all know, the materials’ property can guide the regulation of materials’ structure. In this work, we have investigated the quenching mechanism of Hg2+ to C-dots for a further structural regulation guidance. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, by introducing Hg2+ into the system, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots showed a serious decline. However, upon the addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt, the quenched fluorescence was recovered instantly. It demonstrated that the Hg2+ induced fluorescence quenching of C-dots is a reversible process. To understand the occurred ultrafast process, we have conducted the femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy measurement. When Hg2+ exists, the fluorescence decay rate of C-dots is largely accelerated (Fig. 2B). The double exponential fit result to the fluorescence lifetime of C-dots was displayed in Table S2. As we can see, in the presence of Hg2+, the lifetime of C-dots decreased from 337.8 ps to 138.7ps, suggesting an influence of Hg2+ on the deexcitation approach of excited C-dots. The interaction of Hg2+ and the excited C-dots resulted no photon emissions in the return of excited C-dots to the ground state, which is ascribed to a dynamic quenching process [38]. The transient absorption spectra helped us to learn more about the excited states of C-dots (Fig. 2C and D). Only the excited state absorption of C-dots can be observed and the peak shape and position of the two signals are almost unchanged whether with or without Hg2+, indicating that Hg2+ does not affect the excited-state of C-dots. By this way, the Hg2+ addition only makes the number of deexcitation way of our C-dots increase, while has no influence on the intrinsic deexcitation time of C-dots. As the increase of Hg2+ concentration (Fig. S1), the fluorescence intensity of C-dots gradually dropped. From the Stern-Volmer equation, we can evaluate quenching constant (K SV) of Hg2+ to C-dots to be 3.4 × 104 M-1. Based on the equation of k q = K SV/τ 0, therefore, we can obtain the quenching constant (kq) of Hg2+ to C-dots to be 1.0 × 1014 M-1 s-1. Generally, the value of kq exceeds 107 M-1 s-1 could be ascribed to the process of energy transfer or electron transfer [36]. However, the absorption band of Hg2+ displayed little overlap with the fluorescence emission of C-dots (Fig. S2), excluding the probability of energy transfer between them. Considering Hg2+ with empty orbits has the ability to accept electrons, thereto, an electron transfer process may take place in this quenching event. Besides, the quenching ability of Hg2+ to C-dots was rather high and the distance between fluorophore and quencher required a short, there should be some structural sites on C-dots for Hg2+ to interact with. Finally, the electrons of excited-state C-dots transfer to the empty orbital of Hg2+, leading to a fluorescence decrease of our C-dots.

Since Hg2+ quenched the fluorescence emission of C-dots through interacting with the surface structure on C-dots, we speculated that the other metal ions experimented in Fig. 1F quenching the fluorescence of C-dots were also related to the surface structure. From the structural characterization results, we have known that the C-dots in this work was functionalized with amounts of oxygen functional groups including carboxyl, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. The carboxyl and hydroxyl groups have been reported to have strong coordination ability to metal ions [24,30,39]. The coordination of these groups with metal ions could narrow the distance between C-dots and metal ion and results in an efficient fluorescence quench [38]. Thus, protecting these oxygen functional groups is a good choice to reduce the fluorescence quench from the interfering ions. Here, 1,3-propane sultone was used as a passivation agent to protect carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on C-dots, owing to the weak binding constant of sulfonate with metal ions [37]. The sample of C-dots reacted with propane sultone was expressed as iC-dots. As we can see in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of iC-dots became insensitive to ions of Pb2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Bi3+ and Ag+. In Fig. 3B, the presence of 100 μM Hg2+ quenched 97% of the fluorescence intensity of C-dots, while 92% of the fluorescence intensity of iC-dots was remained. Even as the Hg2+ concentration reached at 1 mM, 87% of the fluorescence intensity of iC-dots was still retained. In short, the passivation of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups has effectively inhibited the fluorescence response of metal ions to C-dots.

To improve the selectivity of C-dots towards Hg2+, apart from the decrease of external interferences, we also need to modify a molecule on C-dots to improve the recognition ability. At present, molecules that specifically recognize Hg2+ can be generally divided into two categories. One is the small molecule, such as anthracene derivatives and rhodamine derivatives, but these molecules usually have poor water solubility. The other one is the biomolecules such as Hg2+ specific whole-cell bacterial, protein, oligonucleotide, DNAzyme and antibody. Among them, oligonucleotide containing multiple thymine moieties has been widely used because of the low cost and easy operation. Meanwhile, the T-rich oligonucleotide is highly selective for Hg2+, because of its specific stabilization ability of T-T mispairs [40,41]. Tortolini reported an electrochemical platform for the Hg2+ recognition via the utilization of T-rich DNA and it was further taken for the evaluation of mercury in fish samples with a recovery in the range of 92–107% [42]. Therefore, a T-rich DNA was selected as a recognition unit to improve the specificity of C-dot towards Hg2+.

Through the structural regulation of C-dots, a selectivity enhancement proposal of Hg2+ by C-dots was put forward. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , C-dots was acyl chlorinated and reacted with DBCO-NH2 to obtain C-dots-DBCO products (the reaction of C-dots with DBCO-NH2 molecules was for subsequent N3-DNA connection as the high reaction efficiency of cycloalkynes and azide groups). Then, 1,3-propane sultone molecule was used as a passivation agent to protect the hydroxyl and residual carboxyl groups and the obtained sample was abbreviated as sC-dots. Last but not least, a T-rich DNA was linked onto sC-dots through the cycloaddition click-chemistry and the final product was indicated as sC-dots-DNA.

The structural transformation of C-dots was characterized by the fourier transform infrared spectra. In Fig. 4 A, after the C-dots functionalized with DBCO-NH2 and propane sultone molecules, the weak absorption band at 2150 cm-1 related to C
≡
C bond appeared. Besides, the peak at 2560 cm-1 ascribed to the hydrogen bond absorption of carboxylic acid vanished and a shoulder peak at 1654 cm-1 ascribed to the amide I band appeared. Meanwhile, bands at the wavenumber of 612, 530 and 1045 cm-1 emerged, which representing the characteristic peaks of –SO3 - group. The full XPS spectra results were presented in Fig. 4B. For sC-dots sample, the increase of nitrogen content as well as the new arising elemental peak of S 2p (164 eV) indicated the connection of DBCO-NH2 and sulfonic acid group, which were consistent with the results of fourier transform infrared spectra. In the proton magnetic resonance spectra (Fig. 4C), compared with the original C-dots (red line), the spectrum of sC-dots (purple line) shows the peaks ascribing to hydrogen between two amides around 3.54 ppm and 2.85 ppm. Besides, the magnitudes of peaks within the range of 6.8–7.8 ppm became stronger, and this is mainly because of the presence of 1H nuclei in the aromatic ring of DBCO. In addition, the new peaks at 2.1, 2.9, 4.4 ppm attributing to the β, α, γ hydrogen of sulfonic acid group arose. All these results revealed that the modification and passivation process were successful.

As shown in Fig. S3A, the sC-dots exhibited a uniform dispersion. The corresponding size distribution of it was depicted in Fig. S3B. The result shows that the sC-dots has an average diameter of 2.1 ± 0.4 nm, which is consistent with C-dots, indicating that the structure modification has little effect on the diameter of C-dots. We have known that passivation by 1,3-propane sultone can protect the fluorescence of C-dots from metal ions (Fig. 3A). To further determine whether if the connection of DBCO-NH2 molecule on C-dots affect the passivation result, the effects of metal ions on the fluorescence of sC-dots was also examined. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence response of sC-dots with metal ions was similar with that of iC-dots, which indicated that the C-dot modified with DBCO-NH2 and 1,3-propane sultone molecules still keeping insensitive to metal ions.

Then, the T-rich DNA was connected onto C-dots through the cycloaddition click chemistry reaction of cycloalkynes group and azides group. Agarose gel electrophoresis was used to verify and separate the product of sC-dots-DNA. In Fig. S4, the migration of sC-dots-DNA was slower than that of sC-dots, because of the relatively large molecular weight of sC-dots-DNA. Then the slower band of sC-dots-DNA was collected and used for the further selective recognition of Hg2+. As pictured in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence intensity of sC-dots-DNA decreased with the increasing concentration of Hg2+ ion. In the range of 1.18–47 μM, the intensity of (I0–I)/I versus Hg2+ concentration displayed a good linear fitting (Fig. S5B), with a limit of detection to be 0.67 μM (3σ/S).

For evaluation the feasibility of our proposed strategy, real samples such as tap water and river water for Hg2+ detection were examined. Before the experiments, the water solution samples were filtered to get the suspended matter removed. Then a defined amount of Hg2+ were spiked into the real samples for recovery measurements. The results are summarized in Table 3 . The recovery was between 90% and 110% and the largest relative standard deviation was less than 5%, indicating that our method is potential to detect Hg2+ in environmental samples.

In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Hg2+ to C-dots was revealed to be an electron transfer process from the excited-state C-dots to the empty orbital of Hg2+. Based on the surface structural rational regulation, we have proposed a reasonable proposal to improve the selective recognition of Hg2+ by C-dots. The inhibition of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on C-dots has reduced the external interference from interfering ions. The linkage of T-rich DNA provides a low-cost specific recognition of Hg2+. The combination of the two effects makes the selectivity performance of Hg2+ by C-dots several orders of magnitude improvement. In a sense, as long as suitable DNA strands or other recognition structures are available, our strategy can be adopted to improve the recognition selectivity of any metal ion by C-dots, which will promote the selective recognition of metal ion in C-dots research.

Mengli Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Cui Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Ying Peng: Formal analysis. Ru-Zhen Xiao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Song Zhang: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Zhi-Ling Zhang: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Bing Zhang: Formal analysis. Dai-Wen Pang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21535005, 91859123, 11974381, 11674355 and 21327804). C. Liu also acknowledges the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805021).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.12.041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design, synthesis, and functionalization strategies of tailored carbon nanodots

Tang, Dual amplification fluorescence assay for alpha fetal protein utilizing immunohybridization chain reaction and metal-enhanced fluorescence of carbon nanodots, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 37606-37614.
